topic: A person discovers the tree they planted as a child has been cut down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of damp earth and old bark hit me first, a phantom memory suddenly made real. I stood at the edge of the clearing, where the sapling I’d nurtured with painstaking care as a child now lay only as a jagged stump. My hands, which had been clasped tightly around the rusted trowel, began to tremble, a faint vibration running up my arms. I sank to my knees, the cool grass seeping through my jeans, and stared at the raw, exposed wood. A sharp, breathless sound escaped my throat, a tiny, wounded noise. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the silence. I pressed my palms against the rough bark, searching for any sign of life, any flicker of green, but there was only stark, brutal emptiness where my little sapling had stood. The world seemed to narrow to that single, devastating void.

[story 2]

It was the sheer audacity of the removal that stole my breath. I walked toward the site, my footsteps heavy and uneven on the path. When I saw it—the cleared patch of earth, the freshly exposed roots—a cold, heavy pressure settled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. My jaw tightened so fiercely I could feel the muscles strain beneath my skin. I stopped a few feet away, my eyes fixed on the stumps, the evidence of what was taken. A low, involuntary moan escaped me, a sound of profound shock. I reached out a tentative finger, brushing against the dry wood, and snatched it back as if burned. "How could they?" The words came out as a harsh rasp, laced with a tremor that shook my whole frame. I turned abruptly, needing to flee the sight, my pace quick and uncontrolled, my gaze darting everywhere but the scene.

[story 3]

I circled the area three times, my gaze sweeping over the barren ground as if expecting the tree to reappear, miraculously sprung from the soil. Each rotation brought a fresh wave of tightness behind my eyes, a prickly heat that made my vision swim slightly. I knelt down again, not touching, just observing the clean, brutal severance where life had once been. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot, slow path down my cheek, quickly followed by another, blurring the edges of the scene. My shoulders slumped forward, a heavy weight settling onto my spine. "It was just a tree," I murmured to the empty air, the tone utterly devoid of inflection, yet thick with an unbearable sorrow. The silence of the woods felt vast and accusing, magnifying the emptiness where my youthful hope had been planted.
